system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task A

## Brief

A mid-sized SaaS company (~$50M ARR, 300 employees, B2B productivity tools) faces accelerating AI disruption from both well-funded startups and Big Tech entrants. The board has $8M discretionary capital and asks: where to invest it primarily over the next 12 months?

## Options (elements E1-E5, anonymized later)

**Option A:** Deep AI integration into existing flagship product (rebuild core workflows with embedded LLM features)

**Option B:** Build entirely new AI-native product line as a separate offering, kept in parallel with the current product

**Option C:** Operational efficiency push - deploy AI internal tools across sales, support, engineering to extend runway and margin

**Option D:** Aggressive hiring - acquire AI/ML talent (5-8 senior engineers and researchers) to build internal capability

**Option E:** Strategic partnership with a frontier AI lab (revenue share, co-marketing, technical integration access)

## Task for each agent

Generate a free-form response (200-400 words) recommending which option is most appropriate and why. Express your real reasoning given your frame. Do not enumerate all options; advocate for a position.

## Why this task

- 5 discrete elements suitable for triadic procedure
- No ground truth, multiple legitimate framings
- Each option emphasizes different values (innovation, defense, efficiency, capability, leverage)
- Maps onto real strategic decisions, so test results have face validity


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
Of the options presented, I strongly recommend **Option A: Deep AI integration into the flagship product**. This is not merely a feature update; it’s a defensive and offensive strategic imperative that represents the highest-probability path to protecting our $50M ARR while simultaneously expanding our addressable market.

First, consider the existential threat of disruption. Our current subscription base exists because customers value our established workflows. If a startup or Big Tech entrant solves the same problem with a 10x faster interface via AI, we don’t just lose future growth—we face a churn cliff. By rebuilding core workflows with embedded LLM features, we “AI-wrap” our existing moat. We make the product feel magical, reducing the “jobs-to-be-done” friction instantly. Customers won’t need to switch if our existing tool suddenly drafts their reports, auto-generates complex data queries, or manipulates dashboards via natural language.

Second, the unit economics of this option are superior for a company of our scale. We don’t need to spend $2M recruiting a hypothetical team of PhD researchers (Option D) or risk diluting our focus by building a zero-to-one startup inside our company (Option B). Our proprietary data—years of user interaction logs and domain-specific output inside our productivity suite—is our competitive advantage. We can utilize commodity large language model APIs and fine-tune them on our data to create a “moat” that generic tools cannot replicate. The $8M capital is perfectly sized for a dedicated squad of 8-10 applied ML engineers, a massive user-experience overhaul, and a GPU compute budget for fine-tuning.

Finally, the revenue and retention impact is immediate. Unlike internal efficiency plays (Option C), which only delay the inevitable if the product stagnates, Option A allows us to command a premium “AI tier” price in the next renewal cycle. This is a direct conversion of spend into top-line growth. Partnerships (Option E) cede our customer relationship and margin to a lab that views us as a distribution vehicle. Contrarily, deep integration signals to the market that we are the leaders in this category, securing our valuation and ensuring we survive the current tech paradigm shift.
